results.possibleIdentity: Everett Aison (born c. 1935) is a multi-disciplinary artist, graphic designer, filmmaker, and educator who was highly active in the New York art and film scenes starting in the late 1950s. He is best known for his work in film title design and movie posters during the 1960s and 1970s, particularly for foreign film imports. His notable designs include the iconic posters and opening titles for the American releases of Akira Kurosawa's 'Yojimbo' and Roman Polanski's 'Knife in the Water'. Aison was a seminal figure at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York City, where he was a co-founder of the SVA Film School and served as its director for nearly 40 years. As a filmmaker, he directed several award-winning theatrical shorts, including 'So Much in Common' (1970), which famously screened before the feature 'Five Easy Pieces'. His career also spanned publishing as the former art director of Grossman Publishers, and literature as a novelist ('Artrage', 2006) and illustrator of children's books such as 'Arthur' and 'The Lion's Island'. His diverse output at the intersection of graphic design, technology, and cinema during the 1960s aligns with the era's experimental organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Aison was a seminal figure at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York City, where he was a co-founder of the SVA Film School and served as its director for nearly 40 years.

His career also spanned publishing as the former art director of Grossman Publishers, and literature as a novelist ('Artrage', 2006) and illustrator of children's books such as 'Arthur' and 'The Lion's Island'.

results.possibleIdentity: Lucio Pozzi (born November 29, 1935, in Milan, Italy) is an Italian-born American multidisciplinary artist known for his refusal to adhere to a single signature style. After studying architecture in Rome, he moved to the United States in 1962 and eventually settled in New York. Pozzi's career is marked by extensive experimentation across various media, including painting, performance, video, installation, and writing. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was active in the New York art scene and was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; archival records from the Getty Museum indicate he was part of the E.A.T. Technical Services Program, which matched artists with engineers for collaborative projects in 1968-1969. He gained institutional recognition in the 1970s, participating in the inaugural exhibition at MoMA PS1 (1976), Documenta 6 (1977), and having one of the first solo exhibitions in MoMA's 'Projects: Video' series in 1978. In 1980, he represented the United States at the Venice Biennale. Pozzi is also the founder of 'New Observations' magazine (1981) and has held prestigious teaching positions at the Cooper Union, Yale University, and Princeton University. He currently lives and works in Hudson, New York, and Valeggio sul Mincio, Italy.
